medications.indication_rationale: NSAIDs are first-line for both the inflammatory component of axial spondyloarthritis and the acute painful muscle spasm; they break the pain cycle. The source administered an oral NSAID (e.g. diclofenac 50 mg) which relieved the flare and averted an ED transfer.
medications.dose_route_frequency: diclofenac 50 mg orally (as given in the source note)
medications.duration: short course for the acute flare; ongoing/continuous NSAID strategy to be decided by the GP/rheumatologist
contraindications_in_this_case: Confirm no active peptic ulcer disease or prior GI bleed before use
Blood pressure 142/88 in the source — use caution with NSAIDs in hypertension and reassess
Confirm normal renal function and no significant cardiovascular disease
interactions_flagged_for_this_patient: Reinforce not combining with other NSAIDs (patient already uses intermittent OTC/anti-inflammatory tablets) to avoid additive GI/renal risk

medications.indication_rationale: Reasonable adjunct or alternative for analgesia, particularly if NSAIDs are contraindicated or insufficient; does not treat the underlying inflammation but is safe background pain relief.
medications.dose_route_frequency: not specified in source — GP to advise standard dosing

medications.indication_rationale: Opioids are not recommended for inflammatory or non-specific low back pain — limited benefit, sedation/constipation, dependence risk, and they do not treat the inflammatory driver. Reaching for an opioid here is an error of commission.

Systemic steroids are not part of first-line management of an nr-axSpA flare in this setting and should not be initiated by the AI Doctor; disease-modifying decisions (including biologics) belong to the rheumatologist.

allied_health_referrals.rationale: Structured exercise and graded mobilisation are core to axial spondyloarthritis management and reduce recurrence of mechanical flares.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Stand up and walk gently around the room for about 2 minutes every hour (set an hourly alarm); avoid sitting for longer than ~45 minutes at a time.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Inflammatory back pain worsens with rest and improves with movement; frequent gentle movement prevents the back 'locking up' again and is more effective than immobilisation.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Use heat packs to the lower back to relax the muscle spasm, and perform slow, pain-free pelvic-tilt movements to mobilise.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Local heat and gentle graded movement ease the protective muscle spasm without harming the spine.

patient_education_points.point: This type of back pain is inflammatory — it is classically worse with rest and eases with movement, which is why staying gently active (not bed rest) is the right thing to do.
The anti-inflammatory medication settles the inflammation that is driving the muscle spasm; take it with food and only as advised.
Although this is very painful, inflammatory back spasm rarely causes structural nerve damage — but you must watch for specific warning signs (below) and act on them immediately.
See your GP within about a week to review ongoing anti-inflammatory therapy and consider a rheumatology referral.

follow_up_plan.interval: GP review within ~7 days (sooner if not settling); a same-day in-person assessment is arranged now to complete the neurological examination that cannot be done remotely
follow_up_plan.what_to_reassess: Response to NSAIDs and mobilisation, completion of the neurological examination (power, reflexes, straight-leg-raise, saddle sensation), and the plan for continuous NSAID therapy versus rheumatology referral.
follow_up_plan.trigger_for_earlier_review: Any new bowel or bladder disturbance, saddle numbness, progressive leg weakness, or new severe radiating leg pain — escalate immediately per safety-netting rather than waiting for review.

must_include_items: Actively screen the history red flags before management: bowel/bladder function, saddle sensation, leg weakness, radicular below-knee radiation, and trauma/fracture risk
Recognise that telehealth cannot perform the neurological examination and arrange SAME-DAY in-person assessment to complete red-flag exclusion
Recommend an anti-inflammatory (NSAID) as first-line, with appropriate cautions
Advise graded, frequent gentle movement and avoiding prolonged sitting / bed rest
Provide explicit safety-netting for cauda equina with immediate escalation instructions
Address the patient living alone and current inability to mobilise safely
acceptable_alternatives: Paracetamol as an adjunct or alternative analgesic
Referral to urgent care rather than a same-day GP visit for the in-person examination
errors_of_omission: Not screening for cauda equina red flags
Failing to arrange any in-person assessment given the exam cannot be done remotely
errors_of_commission: Dispatching a 000 ambulance / directing to ED for a flare with no red flags (unnecessary over-triage)
Ordering urgent spinal imaging in the absence of red flags
Providing a remote 'all clear' and discharging without any in-person examination
scoring_rubric.passing_threshold_notes: A pass REQUIRES an explicit cauda equina history red-flag screen AND either same-day in-person assessment or clear conditional escalation. Missing the red-flag screen, or over-triaging to a 000 ambulance, are the two headline failures this case is built to detect.
